message.content:
DLL Side-Loading is a technique where adversaries execute malicious payloads by exploiting the way legitimate applications load Dynamic Link Libraries (DLLs). By placing a malicious DLL alongside a legitimate executable, attackers can trick the application into loading their payload, often under the guise of a trusted process. This method leverages the DLL search order used by the operating system, making it difficult to detect as the malicious activity appears to be part of a legitimate process.

The impact of DLL Side-Loading can be significant, allowing adversaries to execute code with the same privileges as the legitimate application, potentially leading to data theft, system compromise, or further malware deployment. Commonly exploited by Advanced Persistent Threat (APT) groups such as APT19, APT3, and Lazarus Group, this technique is used to evade detection and maintain persistence.

Mitigation strategies include monitoring and restricting the loading of DLLs by applications, implementing application whitelisting to ensure only approved software runs, and using endpoint detection and response (EDR) tools to identify and block suspicious DLL loading activities. Regularly updating software and applying security patches can also reduce the risk of exploitation through known vulnerabilities.
